**Java** is a [high-level](/wiki/High-level_programming_language "High-level programming language"), [general-purpose](/wiki/General-purpose_programming_language "General-purpose programming language"), [memory-safe](/wiki/Memory_safety "Memory safety"), [object-oriented](/wiki/Object-oriented_programming "Object-oriented programming") [programming language](/wiki/Programming_language "Programming language"). It is intended to let [programmers](/wiki/Programmer "Programmer") *write once, run anywhere* ([WORA](/wiki/Write_once,_run_anywhere "Write once, run anywhere")),[[17]](#cite_note-17) meaning that [compiled](/wiki/Compiler "Compiler") Java code can run on all platforms that support Java without the need to recompile.[[18]](#cite_note-design_goals-18) Java applications are typically compiled to [bytecode](/wiki/Java_bytecode "Java bytecode") that can run on any [Java virtual machine](/wiki/Java_virtual_machine "Java virtual machine") (JVM) regardless of the underlying [computer architecture](/wiki/Computer_architecture "Computer architecture"). The [syntax](/wiki/Syntax_(programming_languages) "Syntax (programming languages)") of Java is similar to [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)") and [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++"), but has fewer [low-level](/wiki/Low-level_programming_language "Low-level programming language") facilities than either of them. The Java runtime provides dynamic capabilities (such as [reflection](/wiki/Reflective_programming "Reflective programming") and runtime code modification) that are typically not available in traditional compiled languages.

Java gained popularity shortly after its release, and has been a popular programming language since then.[[19]](#cite_note-19) Java was the third most popular programming language in 2022[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Java_(programming_language)&action=edit) according to [GitHub](/wiki/GitHub "GitHub").[[20]](#cite_note-20) Although still widely popular, there has been a gradual decline in use of Java in recent years with [other languages using JVM](/wiki/List_of_JVM_languages "List of JVM languages") gaining popularity.[[21]](#cite_note-:0-21)

Java was designed by [James Gosling](/wiki/James_Gosling "James Gosling") at [Sun Microsystems](/wiki/Sun_Microsystems "Sun Microsystems"). It was released in May 1995 as a core component of Sun's [Java platform](/wiki/Java_(software_platform) "Java (software platform)"). The original and [reference implementation](/wiki/Reference_implementation "Reference implementation") Java [compilers](/wiki/Compiler "Compiler"), virtual machines, and [class libraries](/wiki/Library_(computing) "Library (computing)") were released by Sun under [proprietary licenses](/wiki/Proprietary_license "Proprietary license"). As of May 2007, in compliance with the specifications of the [Java Community Process](/wiki/Java_Community_Process "Java Community Process"), Sun had [relicensed](/wiki/Software_relicensing "Software relicensing") most of its Java technologies under the [GPL-2.0-only](/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License "GNU General Public License") license. [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation"), which bought Sun in 2010, offers its own [HotSpot](/wiki/HotSpot "HotSpot") Java virtual machine. However, the official [reference implementation](/wiki/Reference_implementation "Reference implementation") is the [OpenJDK](/wiki/OpenJDK "OpenJDK") JVM, which is open-source software used by most developers and is the default JVM for almost all [Linux distributions](/wiki/Linux_distribution "Linux distribution").

[Java 26](/wiki/Java_version_history "Java version history") is the current version as of March 2026[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Java_(programming_language)&action=edit). Java 8, 11, 17, 21, and 25 are [long-term support](/wiki/Long-term_support "Long-term support") versions still under maintenance.

## History

See also: [Java (software platform) § History](/wiki/Java_(software_platform)#History "Java (software platform)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5d/Duke_%28Java_mascot%29_waving.svg/250px-Duke_%28Java_mascot%29_waving.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Duke_(Java_mascot)_waving.svg)

[Duke](/wiki/Duke_(mascot) "Duke (mascot)"), the Java mascot

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/14/James_Gosling_2008.jpg/250px-James_Gosling_2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Gosling_2008.jpg)

[James Gosling](/wiki/James_Gosling "James Gosling"), the creator of Java, in 2008

[James Gosling](/wiki/James_Gosling "James Gosling"), Mike Sheridan, and [Patrick Naughton](/w/index.php?title=Patrick_Naughton&action=edit&redlink=1 "Patrick Naughton (page does not exist)") initiated the Java language project in June 1991.[[22]](#cite_note-22) Java was originally designed for interactive television, but it was too advanced for the digital cable television industry at the time.[[23]](#cite_note-23) The language was initially called *[Oak](/wiki/Oak_(programming_language) "Oak (programming language)")* after an [oak](/wiki/Oak "Oak") tree that stood outside Gosling's office. Later the project went by the name *Green* and was finally renamed *Java*, from [Java coffee](/wiki/Java_coffee "Java coffee"), a type of coffee from [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").[[24]](#cite_note-24) Gosling designed Java with a [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)")/[C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++")-style syntax that system and application programmers would find familiar.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

Sun Microsystems released the first public implementation as Java 1.0 in 1996.[[26]](#cite_note-oraclejavahistory-26) It promised [write once, run anywhere](/wiki/Write_once,_run_anywhere "Write once, run anywhere") (WORA) functionality, providing no-cost run-times on popular [platforms](/wiki/Computing_platform "Computing platform"). Fairly secure and featuring configurable security, it allowed network- and file-access restrictions. Major [web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") soon incorporated the ability to run [Java applets](/wiki/Java_applet "Java applet") within web pages, and Java quickly became popular. The Java 1.0 compiler was re-written [in Java](/wiki/Bootstrapping_(compilers) "Bootstrapping (compilers)") by [Arthur van Hoff](/wiki/Arthur_van_Hoff "Arthur van Hoff") to comply strictly with the Java 1.0 language specification.[[27]](#cite_note-27) With the advent of Java 2 (released initially as J2SE 1.2 in December 1998 – 1999), new versions had multiple configurations built for different types of platforms. [J2EE](/wiki/Java_2_Enterprise_Edition "Java 2 Enterprise Edition") included technologies and APIs for enterprise applications typically run in server environments, while J2ME featured APIs optimized for mobile applications. The desktop version was renamed J2SE. In 2006, for marketing purposes, Sun renamed new J2 versions as *[Java EE](/wiki/Java_Platform,_Enterprise_Edition "Java Platform, Enterprise Edition")*, *[Java ME](/wiki/Java_Platform,_Micro_Edition "Java Platform, Micro Edition")*, and *[Java SE](/wiki/Java_Platform,_Standard_Edition "Java Platform, Standard Edition")*, respectively.

In 1997, Sun Microsystems approached the [ISO/IEC JTC 1](/wiki/ISO/IEC_JTC_1 "ISO/IEC JTC 1") standards body and later the [Ecma International](/wiki/Ecma_International "Ecma International") to formalize Java, but it soon withdrew from the process.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29)[[30]](#cite_note-30) Java remains a [*de facto* standard](/wiki/De_facto_standard "De facto standard"), controlled through the [Java Community Process](/wiki/Java_Community_Process "Java Community Process").[[31]](#cite_note-31) At one time, Sun made most of its Java implementations available without charge, despite their [proprietary software](/wiki/Proprietary_software "Proprietary software") status. Sun generated revenue from Java through the selling of licenses for specialized products such as the Java Enterprise System.

On November 13, 2006, Sun released much of its Java virtual machine (JVM) as [free and open-source software](/wiki/Free_and_open-source_software "Free and open-source software") (FOSS), under the terms of the [GPL-2.0-only](/wiki/GNU_General_Public_License "GNU General Public License") license. On May 8, 2007, Sun finished the process, making all of its JVM's core code available under [free software](/wiki/Free_software "Free software")/open-source distribution terms, aside from a small portion of code to which Sun did not hold the copyright.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

Sun's vice-president Rich Green said that Sun's ideal role with regard to Java was as an *evangelist*.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Following [Oracle Corporation](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation")'s acquisition of Sun Microsystems in 2009–10, Oracle has described itself as the steward of Java technology with a relentless commitment to fostering a community of participation and transparency.[[34]](#cite_note-34) This did not prevent Oracle from filing a lawsuit against Google shortly after that for using Java inside the [Android SDK](/wiki/Android_SDK "Android SDK") (see the *[Android](#Android)* section).

On April 2, 2010, James Gosling resigned from [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation").[[35]](#cite_note-35)

In January 2016, Oracle announced that Java run-time environments based on JDK 9 will discontinue the browser plugin.[[36]](#cite_note-36)

Java software runs on most devices from laptops to [data centers](/wiki/Data_center "Data center"), [game consoles](/wiki/Video_game_console "Video game console") to scientific [supercomputers](/wiki/Supercomputer "Supercomputer").[[37]](#cite_note-37)

[Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation") (and others) highly recommend uninstalling outdated and unsupported versions of Java, due to unresolved security issues in older versions.[[38]](#cite_note-38)

### Principles

There were five primary goals in creating the Java language:[[18]](#cite_note-design_goals-18)

1. It must be simple, [object-oriented](/wiki/Object-oriented_programming "Object-oriented programming"), and familiar.
2. It must be [robust](/wiki/Robustness_(computer_science) "Robustness (computer science)") and secure.
3. It must be architecture-neutral and portable.
4. It must execute with high performance.
5. It must be [interpreted](/wiki/Interpreter_(computing) "Interpreter (computing)"), [threaded](/wiki/Thread_(computing) "Thread (computing)"), and [dynamic](/wiki/Dynamic_programming_language "Dynamic programming language").

## Execution system

### Java JVM and bytecode

Main articles: [Java (software platform)](/wiki/Java_(software_platform) "Java (software platform)") and [Java virtual machine](/wiki/Java_virtual_machine "Java virtual machine")

One design goal of Java is [portability](/wiki/Software_portability "Software portability"), which means that programs written for the Java platform must run similarly on any combination of hardware and operating system with adequate run time support. This is achieved by compiling the Java language code to an intermediate representation called [Java bytecode](/wiki/Java_bytecode "Java bytecode"), instead of directly to architecture-specific [machine code](/wiki/Machine_code "Machine code"). Java bytecode instructions are analogous to machine code, but they are intended to be executed by a [virtual machine](/wiki/Virtual_machine "Virtual machine") (VM) written specifically for the host hardware. [End-users](/wiki/End_user "End user") commonly use a [Java Runtime Environment](/wiki/Java_virtual_machine "Java virtual machine") (JRE) installed on their device for standalone Java applications or a web browser for [Java applets](/wiki/Java_applet "Java applet").

Standard libraries provide a generic way to access host-specific features such as graphics, [threading](/wiki/Thread_(computing) "Thread (computing)"), and [networking](/wiki/Computer_network "Computer network").

The use of universal bytecode makes porting simple. However, the overhead of [interpreting](/wiki/Interpreter_(computing) "Interpreter (computing)") bytecode into machine instructions made interpreted programs almost always run more slowly than native [executables](/wiki/Executable "Executable"). [Just-in-time](/wiki/Just-in-time_compilation "Just-in-time compilation") (JIT) compilers that compile byte-codes to machine code during runtime were introduced from an early stage. Java's Hotspot compiler is actually two compilers in one, with [GraalVM](/wiki/GraalVM "GraalVM") (included in e.g. Java 11, but removed as of Java 16) allowing [tiered compilation](/w/index.php?title=Tiered_compilation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Tiered compilation (page does not exist)").[[52]](#cite_note-52) Java itself is platform-independent and is adapted to the particular platform it is to run on by a [Java virtual machine](/wiki/Java_virtual_machine "Java virtual machine") (JVM), which translates the [Java bytecode](/wiki/Java_bytecode "Java bytecode") into the platform's machine language.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

#### Performance

Main article: [Java performance](/wiki/Java_performance "Java performance")

Programs written in Java have a reputation for being slower and requiring more memory than those written in [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++").[[54]](#cite_note-Jelovic-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) However, Java programs' execution speed improved significantly with the introduction of [just-in-time compilation](/wiki/Just-in-time_compilation "Just-in-time compilation") in 1997/1998 for [Java 1.1](/wiki/Java_version_history "Java version history"),[[56]](#cite_note-56) the addition of language features supporting better code analysis (such as inner classes, the StringBuilder class, optional assertions, etc.), and optimizations in the Java virtual machine, such as [HotSpot](/wiki/HotSpot "HotSpot") becoming Sun's default JVM in 2000. With Java 1.5, the performance was improved with the addition of the `java.util.concurrent` package, including [lock-free](/wiki/Lock-free "Lock-free") implementations of the [ConcurrentMaps](/wiki/Java_ConcurrentMap "Java ConcurrentMap") and other multi-core collections, and it was improved further with Java 1.6.

### Non-JVM

Some platforms offer direct hardware support for Java; there are micro controllers that can run Java bytecode in hardware instead of a software Java virtual machine,[[57]](#cite_note-57) and some [ARM](/wiki/ARM_architecture_family "ARM architecture family")-based processors could have hardware support for executing Java bytecode through their [Jazelle](/wiki/Jazelle "Jazelle") option, though support has mostly been dropped in current implementations of ARM.

### Automatic memory management

Java uses an [automatic garbage collector](/wiki/Garbage_collection_(computer_science) "Garbage collection (computer science)") to manage memory in the [object lifecycle](/wiki/Object_lifetime "Object lifetime"). The programmer determines when objects are created, and the Java runtime is responsible for recovering the memory once objects are no longer in use. Once no references to an object remain, the [unreachable memory](/wiki/Unreachable_memory "Unreachable memory") becomes eligible to be freed automatically by the garbage collector. Something similar to a [memory leak](/wiki/Memory_leak "Memory leak") may still occur if a programmer's code holds a reference to an object that is no longer needed, typically when objects that are no longer needed are stored in containers that are still in use.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloch201826-28§Item_7:_Eliminate_obsolete_object_references-58) If methods for a non-existent object are called, a [null pointer](/wiki/Null_pointer "Null pointer") exception is thrown.[[59]](#cite_note-59)[[60]](#cite_note-60)

One of the ideas behind Java's automatic memory management model is that programmers can be spared the burden of having to perform manual memory management. In some languages, memory for the creation of objects is implicitly allocated on the [stack](/wiki/Stack_(abstract_data_type) "Stack (abstract data type)") or explicitly allocated and deallocated from the [heap](/wiki/Memory_management#DYNAMIC "Memory management"). In the latter case, the responsibility of managing memory resides with the programmer. If the program does not deallocate an object, a [memory leak](/wiki/Memory_leak "Memory leak") occurs.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloch201826-28§Item_7:_Eliminate_obsolete_object_references-58) If the program attempts to access or deallocate memory that has already been deallocated, the result is undefined and difficult to predict, and the program is likely to become unstable or crash. This can be partially remedied by the use of [smart pointers](/wiki/Smart_pointer "Smart pointer"), but these add overhead and complexity. Garbage collection does not prevent [logical memory](/wiki/Logical_address "Logical address") leaks, i.e. those where the memory is still referenced but never used.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloch201826-28§Item_7:_Eliminate_obsolete_object_references-58)

Garbage collection may happen at any time. Ideally, it will occur when a program is idle. It is guaranteed to be triggered if there is insufficient free memory on the heap to allocate a new object; this can cause a program to stall momentarily. Explicit memory management is not possible in Java, however it is possible to make the JVM do garbage collection manually[[61]](#cite_note-61).

Java does not support C/C++ style [pointer arithmetic](/wiki/Pointer_(computer_programming) "Pointer (computer programming)"),[[62]](#cite_note-62) where object addresses can be arithmetically manipulated (e.g. by adding or subtracting an offset). This allows the garbage collector to relocate referenced objects and ensures type safety and security.

As in C++ and some other object-oriented languages, variables of Java's [primitive data types](/wiki/Primitive_data_type "Primitive data type") are either stored directly in fields (for objects) or on the [stack](/wiki/Stack-based_memory_allocation "Stack-based memory allocation") (for methods) rather than on the heap, as is commonly true for non-primitive data types (but see [escape analysis](/wiki/Escape_analysis "Escape analysis")). This was a conscious decision by Java's designers for performance reasons.

Java contains multiple types of garbage collectors. Since Java 9, HotSpot uses the [Garbage First Garbage Collector](/wiki/Garbage-first_collector "Garbage-first collector") (G1GC) as the default.[[63]](#cite_note-63) However, there are also several other garbage collectors that can be used to manage the heap, such as the Z Garbage Collector (ZGC) introduced in Java 11, and Shenandoah GC, introduced in Java 12 but unavailable in Oracle-produced OpenJDK builds. Shenandoah is instead available in third-party builds of OpenJDK, such as [Eclipse Temurin](/wiki/Adoptium#Eclipse_Temurin "Adoptium"). For most applications in Java, G1GC is sufficient. In prior versions of Java, such as Java 8, the [Parallel Garbage Collector](https://docs.oracle.com/javase/8/docs/technotes/guides/vm/gctuning/parallel.html) was used as the default garbage collector.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Having solved the memory management problem does not relieve the programmer of the burden of handling properly other kinds of resources, like network or database connections, file handles, etc., especially in the presence of exceptions.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

## Syntax

Main article: [Java syntax](/wiki/Java_syntax "Java syntax")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7f/JavaUniverse.png/250px-JavaUniverse.png)](/wiki/File:JavaUniverse.png)

This dependency graph of the Java Core classes was created with jdeps and [Gephi](/wiki/Gephi "Gephi").

The syntax of Java is largely influenced by [C++](/wiki/C%2B%2B "C++") and [C](/wiki/C_(programming_language) "C (programming language)"). Unlike C++, which combines the syntax for structured, generic, and object-oriented programming, Java was built almost exclusively as an object-oriented language.[[18]](#cite_note-design_goals-18) All code is written inside classes, and every data item is an object, with the exception of the primitive data types, (i.e. integers, floating-point numbers, [boolean values](/wiki/Boolean_data_type "Boolean data type"), and characters), which are not objects for performance reasons.

Unlike C++, Java does not support [operator overloading](/wiki/Operator_overloading "Operator overloading")[[64]](#cite_note-msdn_operator_overloading-64) or [multiple inheritance](/wiki/Multiple_inheritance "Multiple inheritance") for classes, though multiple inheritance is supported for [interfaces](/wiki/Interface_(Java) "Interface (Java)").[[65]](#cite_note-oracle_multiple_inheritance-65)

Java uses [comments](/wiki/Comment_(computer_programming) "Comment (computer programming)") similar to those of C++. There are three different styles of comments: a single line style marked with two slashes (`//`), a multiple line style opened with `/*` and closed with `*/`, and the [Javadoc](/wiki/Javadoc "Javadoc") commenting style opened with `/**` and closed with `*/`. The Javadoc style of commenting allows the user to run the Javadoc executable to create documentation for the program and can be read by some [integrated development environments](/wiki/Integrated_development_environment "Integrated development environment") (IDEs) such as [Eclipse](/wiki/Eclipse_(software) "Eclipse (software)") to allow developers to access documentation within the IDE.

### Hello world

The following is an example of a ["Hello, World!" program](/wiki/%22Hello,_World!%22_program "\"Hello, World!\" program") in the traditional Java syntax:

```
public class HelloWorld {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello World!");
    }
}
```

Java 25 introduced a simplified syntax for the main class and main method, which enables users to write small Java programs succinctly:[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[67]](#cite_note-67)

```
void main() {
    IO.println("Hello World!");
}
```

### Applet

Main article: [Java applet](/wiki/Java_applet "Java applet")

Java applets were programs embedded in other applications, mainly in web pages displayed in web browsers. The Java applet API was deprecated with the release of Java 9 in 2017.[[68]](#cite_note-68)[[69]](#cite_note-69)

### Servlet

Main article: [Java servlet](/wiki/Java_servlet "Java servlet")

[Java servlet](/wiki/Java_servlet "Java servlet") technology provides Web developers with a simple, consistent mechanism for extending the functionality of a Web server and for accessing existing business systems. Servlets are [server-side](/wiki/Server-side "Server-side") Java EE components that generate responses to requests from [clients](/wiki/Client_(computing) "Client (computing)"). Most of the time, this means generating [HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML") pages in response to [HTTP](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP") requests, although there are a number of other standard servlet classes available, for example for [WebSocket](/wiki/WebSocket "WebSocket") communication.

The Java servlet API has to some extent been superseded (but still used under the hood) by two standard Java technologies for web services:

* the [Java API for RESTful Web Services](/wiki/Java_API_for_RESTful_Web_Services "Java API for RESTful Web Services") (JAX-RS 2.0) useful for AJAX, JSON and REST services, and
* the [Java API for XML Web Services](/wiki/Java_API_for_XML_Web_Services "Java API for XML Web Services") (JAX-WS) useful for [SOAP](/wiki/SOAP "SOAP") [Web Services](/wiki/Web_Service "Web Service").

Typical implementations of these APIs on Application Servers or Servlet Containers use a standard servlet for handling all interactions with the [HTTP](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP") requests and responses that delegate to the web service methods for the actual business logic.

### JavaServer Pages

Main article: [JavaServer Pages](/wiki/JavaServer_Pages "JavaServer Pages")

JavaServer Pages ([JSP](/wiki/Jakarta_Server_Pages "Jakarta Server Pages")) are [server-side](/wiki/Server-side "Server-side") Java EE components that generate responses, typically [HTML](/wiki/HTML "HTML") pages, to [HTTP](/wiki/HTTP "HTTP") requests from [clients](/wiki/Client_(computing) "Client (computing)"). JSPs embed Java code in an HTML page by using the special [delimiters](/wiki/Delimiter "Delimiter") `<%` and `%>`. A JSP is compiled to a Java *servlet*, a Java application in its own right, the first time it is accessed. After that, the generated servlet creates the response.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

### Swing application

Main article: [Swing (Java)](/wiki/Swing_(Java) "Swing (Java)")

[Swing](/wiki/Swing_(Java) "Swing (Java)") is a graphical user interface [library](/wiki/Library_(computing) "Library (computing)") for the Java SE platform. It is possible to specify a different look and feel through the [pluggable look and feel](/wiki/Pluggable_look_and_feel "Pluggable look and feel") system of Swing. Clones of [Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"), [GTK+](/wiki/GTK%2B "GTK+"), and [Motif](/wiki/Motif_(software) "Motif (software)") are supplied by Sun. [Apple](/wiki/Apple_Inc. "Apple Inc.") also provides an [Aqua](/wiki/Aqua_(user_interface) "Aqua (user interface)") look and feel for [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS"). Where prior implementations of these looks and feels may have been considered lacking, Swing in Java SE 6 addresses this problem by using more native [GUI widget](/wiki/Graphical_control_element "Graphical control element") drawing routines of the underlying platforms.[[71]](#cite_note-71)

### JavaFX application

Main article: [JavaFX](/wiki/JavaFX "JavaFX")

[JavaFX](/wiki/JavaFX "JavaFX") is a [software platform](/wiki/Computing_platform "Computing platform") for creating and delivering [desktop applications](/wiki/Application_software "Application software"), as well as [rich web applications](/wiki/Rich_web_application "Rich web application") that can run across a wide variety of devices. JavaFX is intended to replace [Swing](/wiki/Swing_(Java) "Swing (Java)") as the standard [graphical user interface](/wiki/Graphical_user_interface "Graphical user interface") (GUI) library for [Java SE](/wiki/Java_Platform,_Standard_Edition "Java Platform, Standard Edition"), but since JDK 11 JavaFX has not been in the core JDK and instead in a separate module.[[72]](#cite_note-72) JavaFX has support for [desktop computers](/wiki/Desktop_computer "Desktop computer") and [web browsers](/wiki/Web_browser "Web browser") on [Microsoft Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"), [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux"), and [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS"). JavaFX does not have support for native OS look and feels.[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### Generics

Main article: [Generics in Java](/wiki/Generics_in_Java "Generics in Java")

In 2004, [generics](/wiki/Generic_programming "Generic programming") were added to the Java language, as part of J2SE 5.0. Prior to the introduction of generics, each variable declaration had to be of a specific type. For container classes, for example, this is a problem because there is no easy way to create a container that accepts only specific types of objects. Either the container operates on all subtypes of a class or interface, usually `Object`, or a different container class has to be created for each contained class. Generics allow compile-time type checking without having to create many container classes, each containing almost identical code. In addition to enabling more efficient code, certain runtime exceptions are prevented from occurring, by issuing compile-time errors. If Java prevented all runtime type errors (`ClassCastException`s) from occurring, it would be [type safe](/wiki/Type_safety "Type safety").

In 2016, the type system of Java was proven [unsound](/wiki/Soundness "Soundness") in that it is possible to use generics to construct classes and methods that allow assignment of an instance of one class to a variable of another unrelated class. Such code is accepted by the compiler, but fails at run time with a class cast exception.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

## Criticism

Main article: [Criticism of Java](/wiki/Criticism_of_Java "Criticism of Java")

Criticisms directed at Java include the implementation of generics,[[75]](#cite_note-75) speed,[[54]](#cite_note-Jelovic-54) the handling of unsigned numbers,[[76]](#cite_note-76) the implementation of floating-point arithmetic,[[77]](#cite_note-77) and a history of security vulnerabilities in the primary Java VM implementation [HotSpot](/wiki/HotSpot "HotSpot").[[78]](#cite_note-78)
Developers have criticized the complexity and verbosity of the Java Persistence API (JPA), a standard part of Java EE. This has led to increased adoption of higher-level abstractions like Spring Data JPA, which aims to simplify database operations and reduce boilerplate code. The growing popularity of such frameworks suggests limitations in the standard JPA implementation's ease-of-use for modern Java development.

## Class libraries

Main article: [Java Class Library](/wiki/Java_Class_Library "Java Class Library")

The [Java Class Library](/wiki/Java_Class_Library "Java Class Library") is the [standard library](/wiki/Standard_library "Standard library"), developed to support application development in Java. It is controlled by [Oracle](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation") in cooperation with others through the [Java Community Process](/wiki/Java_Community_Process "Java Community Process") program.[[79]](#cite_note-79) Companies or individuals participating in this process can influence the design and development of the APIs. This process has been a subject of controversy during the 2010s.[[80]](#cite_note-80) The class library contains features such as:

* The core libraries, which include:
  + [Input/output](/wiki/Input/output "Input/output") (I/O or IO)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloch20181–4§ 1_Introduction-81) and [non-blocking I/O](/wiki/Non-blocking_I/O_(Java) "Non-blocking I/O (Java)") (NIO), or IO/NIO[[82]](#cite_note-82)
  + [Networking](/wiki/Computer_network "Computer network")[[83]](#cite_note-83) (new [user agent](/wiki/User_agent "User agent") (HTTP client) since Java 11[[84]](#cite_note-84))
  + [Reflective programming](/wiki/Reflective_programming "Reflective programming") (reflection)
  + [Concurrent computing](/wiki/Concurrent_computing "Concurrent computing") (concurrency)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBloch20181–4§ 1_Introduction-81)
  + [Generics](/wiki/Generics_in_Java "Generics in Java")
  + Scripting, Compiler
  + [Functional programming](/wiki/Functional_programming "Functional programming") ([Lambda](/wiki/Lambda_(programming) "Lambda (programming)"), streaming)
  + [Collection libraries](/wiki/Java_collections_framework "Java collections framework") that implement [data structures](/wiki/Data_structure "Data structure") such as [lists](/wiki/List_(abstract_data_type) "List (abstract data type)"), [dictionaries](/wiki/Associative_array "Associative array"), [trees](/wiki/Tree_structure "Tree structure"), [sets](/wiki/Set_(abstract_data_type) "Set (abstract data type)"), [queues](/wiki/Queue_(abstract_data_type) "Queue (abstract data type)") and [double-ended queue](/wiki/Double-ended_queue "Double-ended queue"), or [stacks](/wiki/Stack_(abstract_data_type) "Stack (abstract data type)")[[85]](#cite_note-collections-85)
  + [XML](/wiki/XML "XML") Processing (Parsing, Transforming, Validating) libraries
  + [Security](/wiki/Computer_security "Computer security")[[86]](#cite_note-security-86)
  + [Internationalization and localization](/wiki/Internationalization_and_localization "Internationalization and localization") libraries[[87]](#cite_note-i18n-87)
* The integration libraries, which allow the application writer to communicate with external systems. These libraries include:
  + The [Java Database Connectivity](/wiki/Java_Database_Connectivity "Java Database Connectivity") (JDBC) [API](/wiki/Application_programming_interface "Application programming interface") for database access
  + [Java Naming and Directory Interface](/wiki/Java_Naming_and_Directory_Interface "Java Naming and Directory Interface") (JNDI) for lookup and discovery
  + [Java remote method invocation](/wiki/Java_remote_method_invocation "Java remote method invocation") (RMI) and [Common Object Request Broker Architecture](/wiki/Common_Object_Request_Broker_Architecture "Common Object Request Broker Architecture") (CORBA) for distributed application development
  + [Java Management Extensions](/wiki/Java_Management_Extensions "Java Management Extensions") (JMX) for managing and monitoring applications
* [User interface](/wiki/User_interface "User interface") libraries, which include:
  + The (heavyweight, or [native](/wiki/Native_(computing) "Native (computing)")) [Abstract Window Toolkit](/wiki/Abstract_Window_Toolkit "Abstract Window Toolkit") (AWT), which provides [GUI](/wiki/Graphical_user_interface "Graphical user interface") components, the means for laying out those components and the means for handling events from those components
  + The (lightweight) [Swing](/wiki/Swing_(Java) "Swing (Java)") libraries, which are built on AWT but provide (non-native) implementations of the AWT widgetry
  + APIs for audio capture, processing, and playback
  + [JavaFX](/wiki/JavaFX "JavaFX")
* A platform dependent implementation of the Java virtual machine that is the means by which the bytecodes of the Java libraries and third-party applications are executed
* Plugins, which enable [applets](/wiki/Java_applet "Java applet") to be run in web browsers
* [Java Web Start](/wiki/Java_Web_Start "Java Web Start"), which allows Java applications to be efficiently distributed to [end users](/wiki/End_user "End user") across the Internet
* Licensing and documentation

## Documentation

Main article: [Javadoc](/wiki/Javadoc "Javadoc")

Javadoc is a comprehensive documentation system, created by [Sun Microsystems](/wiki/Sun_Microsystems "Sun Microsystems"). It provides developers with an organized system for documenting their code. Javadoc comments have an extra asterisk at the beginning, i.e. the delimiters are `/**` and `*/`, whereas the normal multi-line comments in Java are delimited by `/*` and `*/`, and single-line comments start with `//`.[[88]](#cite_note-javadoc_comments-88)

[Oracle Corporation](/wiki/Oracle_Corporation "Oracle Corporation") owns the official implementation of the Java SE platform, due to its acquisition of [Sun Microsystems](/wiki/Sun_Microsystems "Sun Microsystems") on January 27, 2010. This implementation is based on the original implementation of Java by Sun. The Oracle implementation is available for [Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows"), [macOS](/wiki/MacOS "MacOS"), [Linux](/wiki/Linux "Linux"), and [Solaris](/wiki/Solaris_(operating_system) "Solaris (operating system)"). Because Java lacks any formal standardization recognized by [Ecma International](/wiki/Ecma_International "Ecma International"), ISO/IEC, ANSI, or other third-party standards organizations, the Oracle implementation is the [de facto standard](/wiki/De_facto_standard "De facto standard").

The Oracle implementation is packaged into two different distributions: The Java Runtime Environment (JRE) which contains the parts of the Java SE platform required to run Java programs and is intended for end users, and the [Java Development Kit](/wiki/Java_Development_Kit "Java Development Kit") (JDK), which is intended for software developers and includes development tools such as the [Java compiler](/wiki/Java_compiler "Java compiler"), [Javadoc](/wiki/Javadoc "Javadoc"), [Jar](/wiki/JAR_(file_format) "JAR (file format)"), and a [debugger](/wiki/Debugger "Debugger"). Oracle has also released [GraalVM](/wiki/GraalVM "GraalVM"), a high performance Java dynamic compiler and interpreter.

[OpenJDK](/wiki/OpenJDK "OpenJDK") is another Java SE implementation that is licensed under the GNU GPL. The implementation started when Sun began releasing the Java source code under the GPL. As of Java SE 7, OpenJDK is the official Java reference implementation.

The goal of Java is to make all implementations of Java compatible. Historically, Sun's trademark license for usage of the Java brand insists that all implementations be *compatible*. This resulted in a legal dispute with [Microsoft](/wiki/Microsoft "Microsoft") after Sun claimed that the Microsoft implementation did not support [Java remote method invocation](/wiki/Java_remote_method_invocation "Java remote method invocation") (RMI) or [Java Native Interface](/wiki/Java_Native_Interface "Java Native Interface") (JNI) and had added platform-specific features of their own. Sun sued in 1997, and, in 2001, won a settlement of US$20 million, as well as a court order enforcing the terms of the license from Sun.[[89]](#cite_note-89) As a result, Microsoft no longer ships Java with [Windows](/wiki/Microsoft_Windows "Microsoft Windows").

Platform-independent Java is essential to [Java EE](/wiki/Java_Platform,_Enterprise_Edition "Java Platform, Enterprise Edition"), and an even more rigorous validation is required to certify an implementation. This environment enables portable server-side applications.

## Use outside the Java platform

The Java programming language requires the presence of a software platform in order for compiled programs to be executed.

Oracle supplies the [Java platform](/wiki/Java_platform "Java platform") for use with Java. The [Android SDK](/wiki/Android_SDK "Android SDK") is an alternative software platform, used primarily for developing [Android applications](/wiki/Android_application "Android application") with its own GUI system.

### Android

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
|  | This article needs to be **updated**. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. *(February 2026)* |

The Java language is a key pillar in [Android](/wiki/Android_(operating_system) "Android (operating system)"), an [open source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") [mobile operating system](/wiki/Mobile_operating_system "Mobile operating system"). Although Android, built on the [Linux kernel](/wiki/Linux_kernel "Linux kernel"), is written largely in C, the [Android SDK](/wiki/Android_software_development#SDK "Android software development") uses the Java language as the basis for Android applications but does not use any of its standard GUI, SE, ME or other established Java standards.[[90]](#cite_note-90) The bytecode language supported by the Android SDK is incompatible with Java bytecode and runs on its own virtual machine, optimized for low-memory devices such as [smartphones](/wiki/Smartphone "Smartphone") and [tablet computers](/wiki/Tablet_computer "Tablet computer"). Depending on the Android version, the bytecode is either interpreted by the [Dalvik virtual machine](/wiki/Dalvik_(software) "Dalvik (software)") or compiled into native code by the [Android Runtime](/wiki/Android_Runtime "Android Runtime").

Android does not provide the full Java SE standard library, although the Android SDK does include an independent implementation of a large subset of it. It supports Java 6 and some Java 7 features, offering an implementation compatible with the standard library ([Apache Harmony](/wiki/Apache_Harmony "Apache Harmony")).

#### Controversy

See also: [Google LLC v. Oracle America, Inc.](/wiki/Google_LLC_v._Oracle_America,_Inc. "Google LLC v. Oracle America, Inc.")

The use of Java-related technology in Android led to a legal dispute between Oracle and Google. On May 7, 2012, a San Francisco jury found that if APIs could be copyrighted, then Google had infringed Oracle's copyrights by the use of Java in Android devices.[[91]](#cite_note-91) District Judge [William Alsup](/wiki/William_Alsup "William Alsup") ruled on May 31, 2012, that APIs cannot be copyrighted,[[92]](#cite_note-92) but this was reversed by the United States Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit in May 2014.[[93]](#cite_note-cnet_appeals-93) On May 26, 2016, the district court decided in favor of Google, ruling the copyright infringement of the Java API in Android constitutes fair use.[[94]](#cite_note-94) In March 2018, this ruling was overturned by the Appeals Court, which sent down the case of determining the damages to federal court in San Francisco.[[95]](#cite_note-95)
Google filed a petition for [writ of certiorari](/wiki/Certiorari "Certiorari") with the [Supreme Court of the United States](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_the_United_States "Supreme Court of the United States") in January 2019 to challenge the two rulings that were made by the Appeals Court in Oracle's favor.[[96]](#cite_note-96) On April 5, 2021, the Court ruled 6–2 in Google's favor, that its use of Java APIs should be considered [fair use](/wiki/Fair_use "Fair use"). However, the court refused to rule on the copyrightability of APIs, choosing instead to determine their ruling by considering Java's API copyrightable "purely for argument's sake."[[97]](#cite_note-97)
